critic_raw:
This design uses a Bi-LSTM to predict disease severity at the next clinical visit versus logistic regression on static features.

The proposed model is a **bidirectional LSTM**. Bidirectionality means the model reads the sequence both forward (past to present) and backward (present to past). For predicting severity at the next visit, the model should only have access to the sequence of visits up to and including the most recent one — the prediction point. A bidirectional LSTM that processes the full observed sequence before the prediction point is methodologically acceptable only if the sequence used as input does not include the visit being predicted or any visits after it. However, the design describes sequences as '24 visits ordered chronologically' for each patient, without explicitly specifying that the last visit in the input sequence represents the most recent historical visit rather than the visit whose severity is being predicted. If the sequence includes the target visit itself (a sequence-to-sequence framing), the backward LSTM reads forward from the target label context backward through prior visits — this is direct label leakage. The design must specify exactly what visit is the prediction target and what the last input sequence element represents.

Mean imputation (described as 'median forward-fill') is applied to within-patient missing symptom values. Forward-fill propagates the last known symptom value forward in time. This is reasonable for slowly-changing lab values but clinically misleading for symptoms that can change rapidly between visits. If a patient's symptoms worsen between visits but no intermediate measurement exists, forward-fill creates a flat trajectory that the LSTM will interpret as stability. The LSTM's claimed advantage is capturing 'symptom progression' — but forward-fill can destroy the precise progression signals (increasing severity trends) that are the model's claimed value-add.

The baseline model is logistic regression (or linear regression) on 'static features only — age, sex, diagnosis, Charlson index, and the severity score from the most recent visit.' Including the most recent severity score as a baseline feature is a strong predictor by itself, due to temporal autocorrelation in chronic disease. The comparison is between the LSTM using a rich temporal sequence vs. the baseline using only the current severity score and demographics. The baseline is intentionally impoverished — it does not include rolling averages, trend features, or any temporal structure. A fair comparison would give the baseline the same temporal features in aggregated form.
all_issues_raised: The bidirectional LSTM reads sequences in both directions; if the input sequence includes or is temporally proximate to the target visit, the backward pass operates on context derived from the prediction target, creating information leakage.

Forward-fill imputation for missing symptom values destroys deterioration trajectories — precisely the signal the LSTM claims to capture — by representing symptom worsening as a flat continuation of the last known value.

The baseline receives only the most recent severity score plus static demographics, while the RNN receives a 24-visit temporal sequence; this is not a fair architectural comparison because the baseline is denied temporal information entirely.
all_issues_adjudicated: The bidirectional LSTM reads sequences in both directions; if the input sequence includes or is temporally proximate to the target visit, the backward pass operates on context derived from the prediction target, creating information leakage.

Forward-fill imputation for missing symptom values destroys deterioration trajectories — precisely the signal the LSTM claims to capture — by representing symptom worsening as a flat continuation of the last known value.

The baseline receives only the most recent severity score plus static demographics, while the RNN receives a 24-visit temporal sequence; this is not a fair architectural comparison because the baseline is denied temporal information entirely.
